text: The laparoscopic view shows the medial umbilical ligament, the vas deferens, and the bladder. The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate the Cooper's ligament, and the pubic tubercle is identified by its hardness.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessel, located just lateral to the medial umbilical ligament. The hernia sac is visible, appearing large and measuring approximately four centimeters.

The surgeon places a Vipro-II mesh using the eye palm technique for laparoscopic hernia repair. The mesh, composed of proline fibers and vicryl, is positioned to cover the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The cecum is visible in the surgical field.

The Vipro mesh is positioned to cover the hernia defect, extending approximately six centimeters beyond its margin. The surgeon uses an absorbable tracker from Covidien to secure the mesh, applying fixation points every two centimeters around the outer edge.

The surgeon supports the mesh with the left hand while positioning the tip of the tracker perpendicular to the surface, firing it around the hernia repair site. In areas such as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster, the surgeon uses a patented suturing technique instead of firing the tracker.

The surgeon navigates above the inguinal ligament, avoiding the bladder and inferior epigastric vessel. The Cooper ligament is visible, and the surgical instrument is fired over it. The rectus abdominis muscle is identified, and firing occurs over this area as well. The medial umbilical ligament on the left side is also targeted for firing. The bladder is deflated to ensure safety during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the rectus abdominis muscle and proceeds to fire over it. The peritoneum is incised, and the cecum is gently mobilized to prevent it from contacting the mesh during the hernia repair.

The surgeon begins continuous suturing using a dandy jamming knot technique, taking a bite with the peritoneum. The rectum is visible, with the ureter located below it. The self-locking knot is applied, requiring careful handling near the rectum. The first bite misses the peritoneum, necessitating a second attempt. The inferior medial end is approached cautiously due to proximity to the rectum.

The surgeon applies the second suture approximately one centimeter from the previous, using a self-locking technique with the suture material. Care is taken to avoid deeper penetration near the ureter and iliac vein, keeping the suture parallel to the peritoneum. The peritoneum over the iliac vessel is visible, showing a split due to prior cecum mobilization. The surgeon considers using fibrin glue or Vicryl suture to secure the area.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the peritoneum on the opposite side, ensuring that the bowel does not lift the inferior edge or enter the defect. Below the peritoneum, an artery is visible.

The surgeon discusses the potential for gas to enter the hernia sac, causing bulging, and recommends scrotal support to prevent CO₂ entry. The use of a mega pore mesh is highlighted, which prevents fluid retention and allows for natural remodeling of the peritoneum. The surgeon performs a locking suture technique in the triangle of pain, ensuring no bowel incarceration, and concludes with an everdrain termination, involving a loop within a loop suture that is locked securely.
